text.title: Product Development Trends (Market-Side): Rules and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Source-freshness, regional-bias guards, kill-list requirement, consumer vs platform separation, and AI-agent failure modes for the market-side trend brief.

rule.statement: Anchor every macro trend to one quantifiable market datapoint — funding round, traffic-share delta, review-count trajectory. Label trends without such an anchor as "watch", not "act".
rule.rationale: "Trend" articles are written for SEO. Without a quantifiable anchor, a trend claim is indistinguishable from a well-cited opinion piece.

rule.statement: Force at least one EU and one APAC primary source per trend or reject the brief as regionally biased. Never blend US + EU + APAC CPC into one number.
rule.rationale: LLMs over-index on US/SF sources; training data overweights US benchmarks. For non-US markets, a US-only brief actively misleads decision-making.

rule.statement: Date-stamp at the bullet level, not just the document. Future agents diff bullet-dates to detect staleness without re-reading the full document.
rule.rationale: Document-level dates hide which specific bullets have gone stale. Bullet-level dates let a quarterly diff agent flag individual claims without a full re-research pass.

rule.statement: Do not run this methodology less than 90 days after the previous run unless a triggering event occurred (funding round, regulatory shift, major launch). Trend churn faster than 90 days is noise.
rule.rationale: Macro market trends do not materially change week-to-week. Running more frequently produces re-confirmation of the last snapshot at significant token cost without new information.

rule.statement: Keep each trend's executive summary to ≤3 sentences. Long entries always indicate weak evidence padded with adjectives.
rule.rationale: Evidence-dense trend claims are short by nature. If you cannot express the market implication in 3 sentences, the evidence is not strong enough to warrant a claim.

antipattern.description: WebSearch ranked by SEO not freshness — sorting by published date is skipped, producing stale results summarized as current.
antipattern.reason: Sort by published date in the signal scraper before summarizing. Freshness of source is the primary quality signal in trend research.

antipattern.description: "Rapid pivots, quarterly cycles" added as an unsourced cadence claim generated from LLM defaults rather than evidence.
antipattern.reason: Treat any unsourced cadence claim as hallucinated. Require a named study (DORA, Stack Overflow) with cohort label before accepting a cadence benchmark.

antipattern.description: Three citations to the same secondary article (HBR, McKinsey blog) inflating confidence for a claim.
antipattern.reason: Multiple citations to the same source ≠ multiple sources. Require independent primary sources; citations to the same base article count as one.

antipattern.description: Human-in-the-loop checkpoint missing before the synthesis lands in .aidocs/product_docs/.
antipattern.reason: Without the gate, hallucinated vendor names and fabricated market data points land in deliverables that get circulated to investors and boards. The checkpoint is non-negotiable.

antipattern.description: Token cost blowup: synthesis on opus with 50+ raw signals exceeds 100k tokens.
antipattern.reason: Pre-filter with the signal-scorer.py script before paying for opus synthesis. A scored and trimmed set of signals produces better output at 20% of the cost.

antipattern.description: Duplicate-content trap: both market and product sibling methodologies produce a file called trend-report.md and one silently overwrites the other.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly namespace files: market side writes to trend-report-market.md, product side to trend-report-product.md. Ask "is the deliverable a market memo or a product memo?" before running.
